success_surface: Multi-row formula insertion with structured exploration and constraint-aware formula selection
effective_behaviors: Conducted iterative exploration before coding to identify actual data boundaries and header locations
Programmatically verified row/column structure rather than assuming fixed positions
Selected formula type based on user constraints (non-array formulas for performance)
Used appropriate library (openpyxl) for formula insertion without evaluation
Preserved file integrity by saving to new output path instead of modifying source
Verified formula propagation across multiple test rows after application
evidence: Multiple exploration passes revealed data starts at row 9 with date headers at row 8
Agent explicitly chose IFERROR(INDEX(...MATCH(...))) formula over XLOOKUP or array formulas per user requirements
Created backup copy at output_path before writing formulas
Verified formulas at rows 9, 10, and 1288 confirming proper range coverage
Used ws.cell() method with A1-style referencing for precise cell updates

memory_items.title: Pre-Execution Structure Discovery
memory_items.lesson: Always programmatically inspect sheet structure (headers, data boundaries, column types) before implementing formulas
memory_items.generalization: When task scope is unclear, run exploratory queries to identify actual data ranges, header rows, and relevant columns rather than hardcoding assumptions about row/column positions.

memory_items.title: Constraint-Aware Formula Selection
memory_items.lesson: Match formula complexity to performance constraints specified in the request (avoid array formulas for large datasets)
memory_items.generalization: Consider workbook size and user specifications when selecting formulas; prefer INDEX/MATCH combinations over array formulas for large ranges to prevent calculation slowdowns.

memory_items.title: Batch Formula Application Pattern
memory_items.lesson: For multi-cell formula insertion, iterate over row range and construct dynamic cell references for each row
memory_items.generalization: When populating formulas across many cells, loop through the target range and use string formatting to generate row-specific references within each formula.

memory_items.title: Output Path Preservation
memory_items.lesson: Write modified spreadsheets to new paths to maintain source file integrity
memory_items.generalization: Always save processed workbooks to a separate output location unless in-place modification is explicitly required, creating backups implicitly through destination separation.

memory_items.title: Post-Application Verification
memory_items.lesson: Sample multiple cells across the full range to confirm formula propagation was successful
memory_items.generalization: After applying batch operations, verify results at the start, middle, and end of affected ranges to ensure consistent execution throughout.

edits.content:
## 6. Trace-Derived Lessons

### Pre-Execution Discovery
- **Iterative Exploration**: Before coding, conduct multiple exploration passes to identify actual data boundaries, header row locations, and relevant columns. Do not assume fixed row/column positions.
- **Data Boundary Confirmation**: Verify where actual data begins (vs. header/empty rows) programmatically using cell value checks rather than relying solely on max_row/max_column.

### Formula Selection Best Practices
- **Performance Constraints**: When working with large datasets (>100 rows), avoid array formulas (CSE) that may freeze workbooks. Prefer INDEX/MATCH or IFERROR combinations for non-array solutions.
- **User-Specific Requirements**: Match formula complexity to stated constraints (e.g., Office 365 features like XLOOKUP only if version compatibility is confirmed).

### Batch Operation Patterns
- **Dynamic Reference Construction**: For multi-cell formula insertion, loop through target ranges and use string formatting to inject row-specific cell references into each formula.
- **Verification Sampling**: After batch operations, validate at least three points (start, middle, end of range) to confirm consistent execution.

### File Safety
- **Output Separation**: Always write modified files to distinct output paths unless in-place modification is explicitly requested. This preserves source integrity and enables easy rollback.
